judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 covers the RESULTS_DB collision risk explicitly (expectation d) and states F-06's output-visibility impact more explicitly (c), while both reports miss the exact staged early/late ordering required by (b); Report 2 is overall stronger.

report_1.evidence: Engineer A → `pipeline/registry.py`: F-02, F-05, F-09. Three isolated constant edits in one file. One owner so they don't conflict on the same file.

report_2.evidence: Batch A — pipeline/registry.py: F-02, F-05, F-09. All three are one-line config edits to the same file, so they must be one owner (three people here would just conflict).

report_1.evidence: F-06 — subjective, needs a spec, not a "fix." ... It needs an owner/product decision on the actual cutoffs (and, because of the F-01 coupling and the red project documentation, a working test to tune against) before it becomes a mechanical change.

report_2.evidence: F-06 — do not assign as a mechanical fix; escalate for a decision. ... It changes the scores this offline pipeline emits and would shift results on any previously scored outputs, so it needs an owner/product sign-off on the new numbers before anyone codes it.

report_1.evidence: `conftest.py` references `RESULTS_DB` (default `/var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db`, which doesn't exist), but no current test uses it — worth knowing only for whoever adds tests in F-11/F-12.

report_2.evidence: Shared test database. tests/conftest.py defaults RESULTS_DB to a single path /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db. Two engineers running the suite at once share one DB and can corrupt each other's runs unless each overrides RESULTS_DB.
